summary: This lesson continues covering irregular preterite stems: poner (pus-), hacer (hic-/hiz-), venir (vin-), traer (traj-). It introduces the shared preterite of ser and ir (fui/fue/fueron) and shows how irregular preterite stems carry over into the past subjunctive. The -i- drops from -ieron in certain verbs (fueron, trajeron, dijeron).

teachings.summary: More irregular preterite stems covered: poner → pus- (puse, puso), hacer → hic-/hiz- (hice, hizo), venir → vin- (vine, vino), traer → traj- (traje, trajo). All follow the same ending pattern: -e (I), -o (he/she), -imos (we), -iste (you), -ieron (they), unaccented.
teachings.rule: Poner → pus-. Hacer → hic- (hizo for he/she, z replaces c before o). Venir → vin-. Traer → traj-.
teachings.notes: Poner → pus- reveals why compound forms use -pose in English (componer/compose, exponer/expose). Hizo uses z instead of c to preserve the /s/ sound before o.

teachings.conceptName: Ser and ir share the same preterite
teachings.summary: Ser (to be) and ir (to go) share the same preterite forms: fui (I was/went), fue (he was/went), fuimos (we), fuiste (you informal), fueron (they). Context determines meaning. Extremely irregular — doesn't follow the standard pattern.
teachings.rule: Fui = I was / I went. Fue = he/she was / went. Fuimos, fuiste, fueron.
teachings.notes: The -i- drops from -ieron in fueron (because 'fuieron' is hard to say). Same happens with trajeron and dijeron.

teachings.conceptName: Irregular preterite stems carry into past subjunctive
teachings.summary: The past subjunctive is built from the 'they' preterite form, so all irregular stems carry over: fueron → fuera, tuvieron → tuviera, hicieron → hiciera, dijeron → dijera, trajeron → trajera.
teachings.rule: They preterite → past subjunctive. Fueron → fuera. Tuvieron → tuviera. Hicieron → hiciera.

examples.spanish: Si lo tuviera, te lo contaría
examples.english: If I had it, I would tell you
I wanted them to do it
I wanted us to do it

teachings.notes: This is why the past subjunctive is built from the 'they' preterite: to carry over irregularities.

teachings.summary: When the irregular preterite stem ends in j or the diphthong would be hard to pronounce, the -i- drops from -ieron: trajieron → trajeron, dijieron → dijeron, fuieron → fueron.
teachings.rule: After j or difficult diphthongs, -ieron loses the i: trajeron, dijeron, fueron.
teachings.notes: The -i- falls away for efficiency of pronunciation.

vocabulary.english: wine; also: he/she came (preterite of venir)
suit (noun); also: I brought (preterite of traer)
